content.value: Ealt is a new concept in minimalist health apps. The concept is for Ealt to be the first app you open as soon as you pick up your phone in the morning, not social, email or games.

The idea is to bring into one app the main applications that a user can and does use to get ready for the day, from the clothes to put on to the physical activity Ealt provides:

- Location
- Date (day, month, year)
- Temperature
- Humidity
- Weather situation (sunny, rainy, cloudy, snow)
- Today Tips: Small phrases that provide advice based on weather situations
- Hours of sleep (yet to be confirmed)
- Steps
- Heart rate
- Height
- Weight

name: iPadOS calculator Concept
description: Concept for a native iPadOS calculator: a swipe-accessible popup calculator designed for quick, seamless use without leaving the current app.

content.value: This is a concept illustrating what the iPadOS native calculator might look like, an idea often mentioned by Apple's senior vice president Craig Federighi. Currently, iPads do not have a native вЂњcalculatorвЂќ app as they do on iOS, as there are many quality alternatives on the market and Apple has not yet developed a single app that meets the requirements for iPadOS.

Instead of devising a full-screen version, I thought of a popup version. This would make the calculator more accessible and easy to use while using other apps. In fact, we often need a calculator while we are already engaged in another activity on the iPad, and having to interrupt that activity to open a separate app is inconvenient.

My prototype works similarly to other iPadOS features, emerging from the corners such as the вЂњquick noteвЂќ feature. Swiping with a finger or the Apple Pencil from the outside to the inside of the screen would bring up the calculator quickly and intuitively.

This is a personally made prototype of an extremely compact indoor security camera. Current solutions available on the market are often relatively bulky, despite the fact that there is no real need for space for electronics. While analyzing a Xiaomi model, I noticed a waste of interior space.
I therefore decided to design a camera with more discreetly camouflaged shapes for the home environment.
Due to its size, it can be easily placed on shelves, bedside tables and furniture without being obtrusive. The design was created using Figma, without the aid of 3D modeling software.

The constraint was to communicate everything through typography and layout alone.
Built with plain HTML and CSS, deployed on GitHub Pages. No frameworks, no build tools.

name: Type Experiments
description: A series of typographic experiments exploring the boundaries of legibility and visual rhythm through modular grid systems.

content.value: A series of typographic experiments exploring the boundaries of legibility and visual rhythm. Each piece uses a strict modular grid as the underlying structure, with type as both content and texture.
Tools used: Figma, Adobe Illustrator. Printed at A2 format for the physical exhibition.
